title: Egg Protein Content Reference
subtitle: Whole Egg vs Egg White — Gram-for-Gram Protein Yields
sourceFile: user-provided content (chat); USDA FoodData Central approximate values

items.title: How much protein is in a whole egg vs egg white?
items.content:
Egg protein reference (approximate):

• 1 large whole egg (~50 g): ~6.3 g protein — includes both white and yolk
• 100 g whole egg: ~12.6 g protein — equivalent to roughly 2 large whole eggs
• 100 g egg white: ~10.9 g protein — equivalent to the whites of about 3 large eggs

Clinical pearl: egg whites are protein-dense with minimal fat and phosphorus compared to whole eggs — relevant when counseling CKD, dyslipidemia, or calorie-restricted high-protein diets. Whole eggs provide more total protein per egg but also yolk lipid, cholesterol, and phosphorus.
keyPoints: 1 large whole egg (~50 g) ≈ 6.3 g protein
100 g whole egg ≈ 12.6 g protein (~2 large eggs)
100 g egg white ≈ 10.9 g protein (~3 large whites)
items.reference: User-provided measurement table; values align with USDA FoodData Central egg composition data.

items.title: When does egg white vs whole egg matter clinically?
items.content:
CKD / DKD: Whole eggs contribute more dietary phosphorus and saturated fat; egg whites are often preferred when targeting high biological-value protein with lower phosphorus load (alongside phosphate binders and dietary counseling).

Dyslipidemia / weight management: Egg whites deliver protein with minimal fat — useful when increasing protein without excess calories.

Malnutrition / sarcopenia: Whole eggs are efficient single-food protein units (~6.3 g each); three large whole eggs ≈ 19 g protein.

Pancreatic insufficiency: Eggs are generally well tolerated; protein content helps meet requirements when fat must be moderated.
keyPoints: Egg whites: high protein, low fat, lower phosphorus
Whole eggs: convenient ~6.3 g protein per egg
Context: CKD, lipids, malnutrition, PEI counseling
items.reference: Clinical nutrition application of user-provided protein yields.

items.question: Approximately how much protein does one large whole egg (~50 g) provide?
items.explanation: One large whole egg (~50 g) contains ~6.3 g protein from both white and yolk combined.
items.reference: Reference table: "1 Large Whole Egg (~50g): ~6.3 grams."

items.question: 100 g of egg white provides approximately how much protein, and equals roughly how many large egg whites?
options: 10.9 g — whites of about 3 large eggs
items.explanation: 100 g egg white ≈ 10.9 g protein, equivalent to the whites of about 3 large eggs.
items.reference: Reference table: "100g Egg White: ~10.9 grams... whites of about 3 large eggs."

items.question: 100 g of whole egg (~12.6 g protein) is approximately equivalent to:
items.explanation: 100 g whole egg provides ~12.6 g protein — roughly 2 large whole eggs at ~50 g each.
items.reference: Reference table: "100g Whole Egg: ~12.6 grams... roughly 2 large whole eggs."

items.statement: Per 100 g, egg white provides more protein than whole egg.
items.explanation: 100 g whole egg ≈ 12.6 g protein vs 100 g egg white ≈ 10.9 g protein. Whole egg is slightly more protein-dense per 100 g because it is concentrated food matter; yolk adds mass but whole egg still edges egg white per gram.
items.reference: Reference table: whole egg 12.6 g/100g vs egg white 10.9 g/100g.

items.statement: Three large whole eggs provide approximately 19 g of protein.
items.explanation: 1 large whole egg ≈ 6.3 g protein × 3 ≈ 18.9 g (~19 g).
items.reference: Derived from 6.3 g protein per large whole egg.

items.question: Why might egg whites be favored over whole eggs in some CKD dietary plans?
items.answer: Egg whites provide high-quality biological protein with minimal phosphorus and fat compared to whole eggs, which include yolk phosphorus and lipids. When targeting protein needs while limiting phosphate load, egg whites are a more efficient protein-per-phosphorus choice — though total diet and binder strategy matter more than any single food.
keyPoints: Egg whites: high protein, lower phosphorus than whole egg
items.reference: Clinical application note; KDIGO nutrition principles.

items.question: How can you estimate daily egg protein intake at the bedside?
items.answer: Use the anchor values: each large whole egg ≈ 6.3 g protein; each 100 g egg white ≈ 10.9 g protein. Example: 2 whole eggs (12.6 g) + 100 g egg white (10.9 g) ≈ 23.5 g protein. For whole-egg-only diets, multiply egg count × 6.3 g.
keyPoints: Whole egg: ×6.3 g per large egg
Quick multiply for meal protein estimates
